professor.name: Dr. Fenwick
professor.text: We've been examining how first-year courses are graded this semester. A number of universities have begun replacing letter grades in introductory classes with a simple pass/fail mark, arguing that it eases the transition to college and lets students take intellectual risks in unfamiliar subjects. Others counter that it removes a useful signal of effort and leaves graduate schools and employers with far less information. Do you think introductory courses should switch to pass/fail grading? Why or why not?

students.name: Amber
students.text: Pass/fail grading makes real sense for the first year. Students are still adjusting, and the fear of a low grade pushes many of them toward easy, familiar courses instead of challenging ones. Removing that pressure would encourage genuine curiosity rather than constant grade-chasing.

students.name: Jack
students.text: I understand the appeal, but I think letter grades still matter. A single pass/fail mark hides the difference between someone who barely scraped by and someone who truly mastered the material. That distinction is exactly what scholarship committees and future advisors depend on.

professor.name: Dr. Okonjo
professor.text: More and more popular tourist cities have recently moved to cap the number of short-term rental listings, such as those advertised on home-sharing platforms. Supporters argue the caps return apartments to long-term residents and help stabilize rents, while critics warn they cut off income for ordinary homeowners and damage the local tourism economy. Should city governments limit how many short-term rentals are allowed in a single neighborhood? Defend your position.

students.name: Diane
students.text: Capping short-term rentals is a reasonable step. When too many apartments are converted into nightly rentals, the long-term housing supply shrinks and rents climb for the people who actually live and work in the city. Protecting residents should outweigh extra tourism revenue.

students.name: Phil
students.text: I see the housing concern, but strict caps feel heavy-handed. Renting out a spare room is a vital source of income for many families facing rising costs. A blanket limit punishes small hosts rather than the large investors who really drive the shortage.

professor.name: Dr. Salcedo
professor.text: We've been discussing anonymity online this semester. Several platforms are now considering a rule that would require every user to verify a real, government-issued identity before posting. Some experts believe this would sharply reduce harassment and the spread of false information, while others fear it would endanger whistleblowers and silence people who rely on anonymity to speak freely. Should social media platforms require verified real identities from all of their users? Why?

students.name: Cameron
students.text: Requiring verified identities would genuinely improve online discussion. People tend to behave far more responsibly when their real name is attached to their words, and much of the cruelty and misinformation we see thrives precisely because accounts are anonymous and essentially unaccountable.

students.name: Nancy
students.text: Mandatory identity checks worry me. Activists, abuse survivors, and people living under repressive governments depend on anonymity for their basic safety. Stripping it away would not stop determined bad actors, yet it would expose the most vulnerable users to serious real-world danger.

professor.name: Dr. Bhattacharya
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about warning labels on sugary drinks. A handful of governments now require bold health warnings on sodas and other sweetened beverages, much like those printed on cigarette packaging. Proponents say the labels quietly nudge people toward healthier choices, while skeptics argue they oversimplify nutrition and unfairly single out one product. Should sugary drinks be required to carry mandatory health-warning labels? Explain your reasoning.

students.name: Leah
students.text: Warning labels on sugary drinks are worth trying. Many consumers genuinely underestimate how much sugar these beverages contain, and a clear, visible warning gives them information to reconsider at the moment of purchase. Even a modest shift in daily habits would benefit public health.

students.name: Ronald
students.text: I doubt labels alone accomplish much. People already know soda is unhealthy, yet they still buy it for the taste and convenience. Singling out one drink also distracts from the broader diet and lifestyle factors that matter far more for long-term health outcomes.
